executive_summary: Today's most impactful finding comes from JAMA Cardiology : a post hoc analysis of the EVoLVeD trial shows that myocardial fibrosis burden predicts adverse outcomes in asymptomatic severe aortic stenosis patients, but — critically — the benefit of early intervention did not significantly differ based on fibrosis severity, complicating the narrative that fibrosis-guided referral can precisely identify who benefits most from early TAVR. Meanwhile, a major JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions cluster features the GASS-TAVR geriatric assessment score for predicting futility after TAVR, a NOTION-3 substudy showing PCI harms frail TAVR patients without benefit, and a landmark TVT Registry analysis of 5,401 mitral valve-in-valve cases revealing that low post-procedural gradients paradoxically predict worse survival. The Evolut Low Risk 5-year isolated-procedure data are published in Annals of Thoracic Surgery , and a troubling Annals study documents that nearly half of the lowest-surgical-risk patients under 65 are receiving TAVR — directly counter to both ACC/AHA and ESC guidelines. For clinicians parsing today's data: the EVoLVeD fibrosis analysis reinforces the trend toward early intervention in asymptomatic AS (now ESC Class IIa) but reminds us that no single biomarker cleanly stratifies treatment benefit. The NOTION-3 frailty substudy adds nuance to the concomitant PCI-plus-TAVR strategy — a win in non-frail patients, a liability in frail ones. The mitral valve-in-valve gradient data upend conventional wisdom and demand a rethinking of post-MVIV echocardiographic benchmarks. And the under-65 TAVR utilization data should concern every Heart Team: practice is outpacing evidence in the very population where long-term durability matters most.

articles.title: Percutaneous Coronary Intervention in Frail Patients Undergoing Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement: A NOTION-3 Substudy.
articles.abstract: BACKGROUND: Frailty is an important predictor of outcomes in patients with coronary artery disease (CAD) and following transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR). The NOTION-3 (Third Nordic Aortic Valve Intervention) trial demonstrated that performing percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) in addition to TAVR reduced the risk for major adverse cardiac events (MACE). Whether this benefit applies to frail patients remains uncertain. OBJECTIVES: The aim of this study was to evaluate efficacy and safety of PCI in frail TAVR patients with CAD. METHODS: NOTION-3 was an international, open-label, randomized superiority trial enrolling patients with CAD and severe aortic stenosis undergoing TAVR. Patients were randomized 1:1 to PCI or conservative treatment. Frailty was assessed post hoc using a calculated frailty score derived from baseline data on symptom-related limitations, daily function, and quality of life. Primary endpoint was a composite of all-cause mortality, myocardial infarction (MI), and urgent coronary revascularization. Safety endpoints included bleeding and acute kidney injury. RESULTS: Frailty data were available for 407 patients (90%), of whom 130 (32%) were frail. During median follow-up of 2 years (Q1-Q3: 1-4 years), PCI reduced MACE in nonfrail patients (15% vs 33%; HR: 0.42; 95% CI: 0.25-0.69; P < 0.001), as well as death of any cause (P = 0.019), MI (P = 0.004), and urgent revascularization (P = 0.005). No differences were observed in frail patients. In contrast, frail patients undergoing PCI had more bleeding events (HR: 2.51; 95% CI: 1.23-5.11; P = 0.011). CONCLUSIONS: In nonfrail patients with CAD undergoing TAVR, PCI lowered the risk for MACE, all-cause mortality, and MI compared to conservative treatment. In frail patients, PCI increased bleeding without clinical benefit. These findings require confirmation in larger prospective studies.

articles.title: A Geriatric Assessment Score Predicting 1-Year Mortality and Functional Decline After TAVR: The GASS-TAVR Study.
articles.abstract: BACKGROUND: A significant proportion of older adults undergoing transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) fail to achieve meaningful clinical benefit at 1 year, highlighting the need for improved risk stratification. OBJECTIVES: The aim of this study was to develop and validate a geriatric score to determine 1-year mortality or functional decline. METHODS: A multicenter cohort study was conducted across 3 high-volume academic centers in Italy from January 2020 to December 2022. Consecutive patients ≥75 years of age with severe symptomatic aortic stenosis evaluated for TAVR were enrolled. Participants underwent a comprehensive geriatric assessment, including basic activities of daily living (BADL) and the Mini Nutritional Assessment-Short Form. The Society of Thoracic Surgeons score was also collected. The primary outcome was a composite of all-cause mortality or significant functional decline (loss of ≥2 BADL or failure to improve if already impaired) at 1 year. A logistic regression model identified predictive factors, and a new risk score was derived in a 66% derivation cohort and tested in a 34% validation cohort. RESULTS: Among 562 patients (median age 83 years, 58.5% women), 78 (13.9%) met the primary outcome over 11 months (Q1-Q3: 10-12 months). Mini Nutritional Assessment-Short Form score, BADL, lower estimated glomerular filtration rate, and elevated pulmonary artery systolic pressure were independently associated with the composite outcome. The derived score showed high discrimination (area under the curve, 0.92; 95% CI: 0.88-0.96). In validation, performance remained robust (area under the curve, 0.87; 95% CI: 0.79-0.95), outperforming the Society of Thoracic Surgeons score. CONCLUSIONS: A geriatric assessment-based model significantly improved the prediction of 1-year mortality or functional decline in older TAVR candidates, offering a valuable tool to refine patient selection and avoid procedural futility.

articles.title: Prognostic impact of conduction disturbance subtypes after transcatheter aortic valve implantation.
articles.abstract: BACKGROUND: Conduction disturbances (CD) are common in patients with aortic stenosis (AS) undergoing transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI). Although various types of CD occur, the prognostic differences among CD subtypes, including transient and persistent CD, have not been systematically evaluated. METHODS: This retrospective two-center study included 1007 patients with AS who underwent TAVI between August 2016 and September 2024. According to Valve Academic Research Consortium-3 (VARC-3) criteria, CD were defined as left or right bundle branch block, intraventricular conduction delay with QRS ≥ 120 ms, high-degree atrioventricular block (including complete atrioventricular block), or new permanent pacemaker implantation. Patients were categorized into 4 groups: pre-existing CD, transient CD (resolved by discharge or within 7 days), persistent CD (present at discharge or beyond 7 days), or no CD. The primary endpoint was a composite of cardiovascular death or heart failure hospitalization up to 2 years. RESULTS: Of the 1007 patients, 234 (23.2%) had pre-existing CD, 112 (11.1%) had persistent CD, 159 (15.8%) had transient CD, and 502 (49.9%) had no CD. During a median follow-up of 645 days, 109 patients (10.8%) experienced the primary endpoint. The persistent CD group had a higher incidence of the primary endpoint than the other groups (log-rank P = 0.023). CONCLUSIONS: Persistent CD, but not transient or pre-existing CD, independently predicted adverse outcomes after TAVI. This study highlights the prognostic importance of CD persistence for post-TAVI risk stratification.
articles.authors: Okita S, Kitahara H, Kuji H, Miyakoda K, Yaginuma H et al.

articles.title: Impact of TAVR Failure Mechanism on Outcomes After Reintervention: From the EXPLANTORREDO-TAVR Registry.
articles.abstract: BACKGROUND: As transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) expands to patients with longer life expectancy, the impact of failure mechanisms on outcomes of TAVR-explant and redo-TAVR remains uncertain. We sought to evaluate outcomes of TAVR reintervention based on the failure mechanism of the index transcatheter aortic valve. METHODS: From 2009 to 2022, 553 patients from 29 centers in the EXPLANTORREDO-TAVR registry underwent TAVR-explant or redo-TAVR for transcatheter aortic valve failure. Patients with endocarditis were excluded. Patients with structural valve deterioration (SVD, N=224 [64.9%]) were compared with those with nonstructural valve dysfunction (NSVD, N=121 [35.1%]), comprising paravalvular leak (86.0%) and prosthesis-patient mismatch (14.0%). Outcomes were assessed at 30 days and 1 year. RESULTS: Mean age was 75.6±9.3 years, with 42% women. There were no differences in reintervention type between groups (redo-TAVR in 58.0% SVD versus 49.6% NSVD; TAVR-explant: 42.0% versus 50.4%; P=0.14). Compared with NSVD, SVD was the predominant mode of failure in balloon-expandable valves (50.7% versus 24.8%; P<0.001), had a longer time to reintervention (50.7 versus 5.5 months; P<0.001), and favored non-balloon-expandable valves at redo-TAVR (56.9% versus 33.3%; P=0.003). Mortality at 30 days and 1 year did not differ significantly between SVD and NSVD for either redo-TAVR (30 days: 3.2% versus 1.7%, P=1.00; 1 year: 18.0% versus 12.0%; P=0.47) or TAVR-explant (30 days: 16.3% versus 12.1%, P=0.63; 1 year: 40.0% versus 29.5%; P=0.39). There were also no differences in risk-adjusted 3-year cumulative mortality between groups (redo-TAVR: HR, 1.30 [95% CI, 0.68-2.46], P=0.43 [ref=NSVD]; TAVR-explant: HR, 1.24 [95% CI, 0.64-2.41]; P=0.53). CONCLUSIONS: SVD and NSVD failures had distinct valve types and reintervention timing, with SVD having a longer time to TAVR reintervention, but the failure mechanism did not impact reintervention type or clinical outcomes.

aortic.commentary:
Evolut Low Risk 5-Year Isolated Procedure Outcomes

Ramlawi et al., Annals of Thoracic Surgery report 5-year outcomes comparing isolated TAVR versus isolated SAVR from the Evolut Low Risk trial. The abstract provided is limited, but this analysis is important because the original trial included patients with concomitant procedures — isolating TAVR-vs-SAVR removes confounders introduced by combined PCI or other interventions. These data join PARTNER 3 and DEDICATE in building the 5-year evidence base for low-risk TAVR, though the ESC 2025 guidelines already shifted the TAVI-preferred threshold to age ≥70 based in part on earlier Evolut follow-up. Notably, SAVR remains preferred below age 70 (ESC) or 65 (ACC/AHA) given unresolved durability questions beyond a decade.

GASS-TAVR: A Geriatric Assessment Score for Futility

Fumagalli et al. (JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions) developed and validated a geriatric assessment-based score predicting 1-year mortality or functional decline in 562 consecutive TAVR candidates ≥75 years across three Italian centers. The composite endpoint occurred in 13.9% of patients. Predictors included nutritional status (Mini Nutritional Assessment), baseline functional dependence (BADL), renal function, and pulmonary artery pressure. The score achieved an AUC of 0.92 in derivation and 0.87 in validation — substantially outperforming STS-PROM. This is a welcome addition to the futility prediction literature, though it requires external validation in non-Italian cohorts and prospective testing before clinical adoption. The accompanying editorial (Mattace-Raso) contextualizes the growing recognition that procedural success is necessary but not sufficient — functional benefit must be the benchmark.

Cerebral Embolic Protection in High-Calcium TAVR

Pawar et al. (JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions) examine the effectiveness of cerebral embolic protection devices during TAVR in patients with high aortic valve calcium burden. Full data are not available in the abstract, but this addresses an important question: whether CEP devices provide differential benefit in the highest-risk anatomy for embolization. Current data on CEP remain mixed (the PROTECTED-TAVR trial showed a signal for clinical stroke reduction but did not reach significance for the primary endpoint), and risk-stratified analyses like this may help identify patients who derive the most benefit.

Conduction Disturbances: Persistent Is What Matters

Okita et al. (International Journal of Cardiology) analyzed 1,007 TAVI patients across two centers and found that persistent conduction disturbances (present at discharge or beyond 7 days) — but not transient or pre-existing ones — independently predicted the composite of cardiovascular death or heart failure hospitalization at 2 years (log-rank P = 0.023). Persistent CD occurred in 11.1% of patients. This has practical implications for post-TAVI monitoring: transient conduction abnormalities that resolve before discharge may not warrant the same level of concern or pacemaker implantation, though prospective validation is needed before changing threshold-to-implant protocols.

Cardiac Tamponade After TAVI: A 12-Year Experience

Hussein et al. (International Journal of Cardiology) report on 29 tamponade events among 2,354 TAVI procedures at a single UK center (incidence 1.2%), with 51.7% mortality at 90 days. The incidence declined from 2.3% (2013–2018) to 0.9% (2019–2025, P = 0.004), driven mainly by elimination of RV perforation through LV wire pacing adoption. Annular rupture remained the dominant mechanism (51.7%) and trended toward highest mortality (66.7% at 90 days). All but one case involved balloon-expandable valves. These data underscore that while tamponade is rare and declining, annular rupture remains a devastating event — reinforcing the need for meticulous CT-based annular sizing, particularly as TAVR moves into lower-risk populations where complication tolerance is lower.

TAVR Reintervention: Failure Mechanism Does Not Predict Outcomes

Penta et al. (Circulation: Cardiovascular Interventions) report from the EXPLANTORREDO-TAVR registry (553 patients, 29 centers) that structural valve deterioration (SVD, 65%) and non-structural valve dysfunction (NSVD, 35%) had distinct profiles — SVD occurred later (50.7 vs 5.5 months), was more common in balloon-expandable valves, and favored non-BEV at redo-TAVR — but neither failure mechanism predicted differential mortality after redo-TAVR or TAVR-explant. Importantly, 30-day mortality for TAVR-explant remained high at 12–16% regardless of mechanism, consistent with prior data establishing explant as a high-risk procedure. Redo-TAVR mortality was substantially lower (1.7–3.2% at 30 days). These data inform lifetime management planning — a central concern of the ESC 2025 guidelines — and support the emerging preference for redo-TAVR when anatomically feasible.

Delayed Coronary Obstruction From Pannus Formation

Perrard et al. (European Heart Journal: Case Reports) describe a rare case of delayed coronary obstruction caused by early pannus overgrowth around a self-expandable THV (Evolut Pro+) just 6 months post-TAVI, causing recurrent NSTEMI. Neither chimney stenting nor explant was deemed feasible; the patient underwent CABG. This case highlights an underappreciated failure mode that may become more relevant as cumulative TAVR implant numbers grow and patients survive longer with their prostheses.

TAVR vs SAVR for Aortic Regurgitation: Early Single-Center Data

Zhang et al. (Cardiology Research and Practice) retrospectively compared 37 TAVR and 33 SAVR patients with aortic regurgitation at low-to-intermediate risk (single Chinese center, 2021–2024). TAVR showed shorter procedure times, less blood use, shorter stays, and zero 30-day mortality versus four SAVR deaths (P = 0.029). Major caveats: This is a tiny, non-randomized, single-center study with no mention of propensity matching or adjustment for selection bias. AR remains "Class IIb for inoperable patients" in ESC 2025, and the JenaValve ARTIST trial (NCT06608823) is the first RCT designed to compare TAVR versus SAVR for AR. Until ARTIST data are available, these results should be viewed as hypothesis-generating at best.

TAVI Expansion in India

ET HealthWorld reports on the expanding role of TAVI in India, framing it as a minimally invasive innovation redefining heart valve care. While TAVI adoption in emerging markets is an important access story, the same guideline principles apply: age-appropriate patient selection, Heart Team decision-making, and realistic expectations about durability in younger populations are essential regardless of geography.

articles.title: Transmitral Gradients and Mortality Following Transseptal Transcatheter Mitral Valve in Valve.
articles.abstract: BACKGROUND: Echocardiographic transvalvular mitral gradients (TMGs) following transcatheter mitral valve in valve (MVIV) are regarded as correlates of clinical outcomes despite limited data. OBJECTIVES: The aim of this study was to examine the association between discharge and 30-day TMGs and mortality, as well as other clinical outcomes, following MVIV. METHODS: The Society of Thoracic Surgeons/American College of Cardiology TVT (Transcatheter Valve Therapy) Registry was used to extract data for all comers undergoing MVIV from August 2015 to March 2024. Adjusted and unadjusted Cox proportional hazards regression models with cubic spline functions were used to explore the relationship between discharge TMG post-MVIV and all-cause mortality. TMG ranges included low (<4 mm Hg), intermediate (4-7 mm Hg), and high (>7 mm Hg). Kaplan-Meier estimates were generated for all-cause mortality and the composite endpoint of all-cause mortality or stroke across TMG ranges (at discharge and at 30 days), with overall curve comparisons performed using log-rank tests. Pairwise comparisons were based on adjusted HRs and corresponding P values from Cox proportional hazards models. RESULTS: The study included 5,401 MVIV patients, with a median follow-up duration of 377 days (Q1-Q3: 58-687 days). Compared with intermediate and high TMG, low TMG had lower invasive cardiac output (CO) (P = 0.0003 and P < 0.0001, respectively) and cardiac index (P = 0.04 and P = 0.0002, respectively). Low discharge TMG was associated with an increased 3-year all-cause mortality hazard compared with intermediate discharge TMG (adjusted HR: 1.52; 95% CI: 1.22-1.89; P = 0.0002) and compared with high discharge TMG (adjusted HR: 1.35; 95% CI: 1.07-1.71; P = 0.01; 896 mortality events for the entire cohort). Discharge and 30-day gradient groups varied and should not be interchangeable. CONCLUSIONS: Following MVIV, TMG <4 mm Hg was associated with lower CO and cardiac index and increased mortality at 3 years compared with TMG 4 to 7 mm Hg and >7 mm Hg. TMG changed significantly from discharge to 30 days. Discharge TMG should not be the sole determination of procedural outcome, valve performance, or the need for optimization following MVIV without incorporating CO.
articles.authors: Abbas AE, Makkar R, Krishnaswamy A, Kapadia S, Kodali S et al.

articles.title: Asian Pacific Society of Cardiology Consensus Statements on the Use of Transcatheter Edge-to-Edge Repair in the Treatment of Tricuspid and Mitral Regurgitation.
articles.abstract: Since the publication of the 2021 Asian Pacific Society of Cardiology (APSC) consensus statements on the use of MitraClip for mitral regurgitation (MR), other transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (TEER) devices have been introduced for the treatment of both tricuspid regurgitation (TR) and MR. Hence, the APSC developed these consensus recommendations, with general cardiologists and internal medicine specialists practicing cardiology as the intended readers, to update the recommendations on the appropriate use of TEER in patients with TR or MR. The APSC expert panel reviewed and appraised the available evidence using the GRADE system. Consensus recommendations were developed and put to an online vote. Consensus was reached when at least 80% of votes for a given recommendation were in support of 'agree' or 'neutral', The resulting 16 statements provide guidance for clinical practitioners in the region on the evaluation and management of patients with TR or MR in the Asia-Pacific region who are being considered for TEER therapy.
articles.authors: Yeo KK, Muller DWM, Wang J, Kubo S, Sung SH et al.

mitral.commentary:
The Gradient Paradox After Mitral Valve-in-Valve

The Abbas et al. TVT Registry analysis (detailed above) is the day's most clinically disruptive mitral finding. A low transmitral gradient (<4 mmHg) after MVIV reflects low cardiac output — not excellent valve function. The accompanying editorial by Pilgrim and Tomii contextualizes this "gradient paradox," arguing that cardiac output must be integrated into post-MVIV assessment. This parallels the well-known low-flow, low-gradient paradox in aortic stenosis and should change how echocardiographers report post-MVIV hemodynamics. The recent Heart Valve Collaboratory framework on imaging transcatheter valve failure is directly relevant here.

5-in-1 Transcatheter Mitral Valve Replacement

Camaj et al. (JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions) describe a combined transcatheter procedure involving mitral valve replacement with ventricular and atrial septal closure using electrosurgery. Abstract details are limited, but this reflects the growing complexity of catheter-based structural interventions and the trend toward addressing multiple pathologies in a single session — an approach that trades procedural efficiency against cumulative risk in what are typically high-risk patients.

APSC Consensus on TEER for Mitral and Tricuspid Regurgitation

Yeo et al. (JACC: Asia) present updated Asian Pacific Society of Cardiology consensus statements on transcatheter edge-to-edge repair for both MR and TR. Sixteen consensus statements were developed using GRADE methodology. This is notable as a regional adaptation effort — the Asia-Pacific region has distinct epidemiology (more rheumatic disease, younger patients, variable access to surgery) that may appropriately shift the risk-benefit calculus for TEER, though the same evidence limitations apply.

tricuspid.commentary:
Real-World Evoque TTVR Data: Reassuring but Early

TCTMD reports that real-world Evoque transcatheter tricuspid valve replacement data are "reassuring" — consistent with the recently published STS/ACC TVT Registry analysis showing 98.4% implantation success, 3.1% 30-day mortality, and significant functional improvement. The headline captures the right nuance: it's still early days. The ESC 2025 guidelines gave transcatheter TR treatment a Class IIa recommendation based on TRILUMINATE, Tri.Fr, and TRISCEND II, but long-term durability and the optimal threshold for intervening in TR (repair vs replacement) remain unresolved. New CIED implantation (15.9%) and bleeding (7.9%) rates in the TVT Registry were actually lower than the TRISCEND II pivotal trial, which is encouraging but may reflect real-world patient selection differences.

Acute Hemodynamic Instability After TTVR

JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions publishes on the frequency and outcomes of acute hemodynamic instability following TTVR — a complication unique to transcatheter tricuspid replacement that has garnered attention as volumes increase. The right ventricle is preload-sensitive, and abruptly eliminating severe TR can unmask RV dysfunction. This remains an active area of investigation and underscores the importance of pre-procedural RV assessment — the ESC 2025 guidelines explicitly exclude patients with severe RV dysfunction from transcatheter TR treatment recommendations.

articles.title: Hospital-Level Variation in Transcatheter versus Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement Among Patients Younger than 65 Years.
articles.abstract: BACKGROUND: Current guidelines do not recommend transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) in adults younger than 65 years with isolated aortic stenosis and a life expectancy >10 years. METHODS: This retrospective cohort study was conducted using the Vizient Clinical Database in adults < 65 years with isolated aortic stenosis who underwent aortic valve replacement with a bioprosthetic valve between 2018 and 2023. Hospital-level variation in TAVR vs SAVR was evaluated using multilevel multivariable logistic regression. RESULTS: Among 13,907 aortic valve replacements, 6,142 (44.2%) were surgical (SAVR), and 7,765 (55.8%) were trans-catheter (TAVR). The median hospital TAVR rate was 52.9% (interquartile range [IQR]: 35.3%-70.7%). Among patients at the lowest surgical risk (predicted mortality < 0.5%), 46.5% underwent TAVR. Patients treated at hospitals with higher risk-adjusted TAVR vs SAVR utilization had a 2.7-fold higher odds of undergoing TAVR than patients treated at hospitals with a lower risk-adjusted rate (median odds ratio, 2.69; 95% confidence interval: 2.43-3.02). Lower-volume hospitals (based on total AVR volume [SAVR plus TAVR]) performed fewer TAVRs vs SAVRs compared to higher-volume hospitals. Teaching status, Disproportionate Share Hospital Percentage, rurality, and average daily census were not significantly associated with TAVR utilization. CONCLUSIONS: Substantial variation exists in hospital rates of TAVR versus SAVR among patients younger than 65 with isolated aortic stenosis, even after adjusting for patient characteristics. Nearly half of the lowest-risk patients (mortality <0.5%) under the age of 65 underwent TAVR instead of SAVR. These findings suggest that practice patterns and non-clinical factors may influence procedure selection in this population.
articles.authors: Glance LG, Dick AW, Knight PW, Bruckel JT, Ling FS et al.

articles.title: Temporal trends in causes, management and outcomes of cardiac tamponade complicating TAVI: A 12-year single-center experience.
articles.abstract: BACKGROUND: Cardiac tamponade is an uncommon but life-threatening complication of transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI). Despite advances in imaging, device design, and operator experience, it remains associated with high morbidity and mortality. METHODS: We retrospectively analyzed all consecutive TAVI procedures performed at a high-volume tertiary center between 2013 and 2025. Baseline characteristics, procedural details, mechanisms of tamponade, and outcomes were obtained from institutional databases. RESULTS: Among 2354 TAVI procedures, 29 cases of tamponade occurred (1.2%). The mean age was 78 years, and 65.5% were women. Balloon-expandable valves were used in all but one case. Annular rupture was the leading mechanism (51.7%), followed by LV and RV perforation (13.8% each), while 20.7% had an indeterminate source. Pericardiocentesis was performed in 90% of cases, and 37.9% underwent sternotomy. In-hospital and 90-day mortality were 41.4% and 51.7%, respectively. Annular rupture showed a trend toward higher 90-day mortality than ventricular perforation (66.7% vs 25%, p = 0.09). Temporal analysis demonstrated a significant decline in incidence, from 2.29% in 2013-2018 to 0.86% in 2019-2025 (p = 0.004). This reduction was mainly driven by eliminating RV perforation through adoption of LV-wire pacing. Mortality improved numerically over time (90-day mortality 64% early vs 40% late), although not reaching statistical significance. CONCLUSIONS: In this high-volume single-center registry, the incidence of tamponade declined over time, driven mainly by a reduction in ventricular perforation. Annular rupture persisted as the dominant and most lethal mechanism, whereas ventricular perforation showed better outcomes. More advanced risk-stratification tools are needed to identify patients at heightened risk.
articles.authors: Hussein H, Elsherif A, Mechery A, Lawton E, Parsley D et al.

articles.title: Myocardial Fibrosis and Early Intervention in Asymptomatic Patients With Severe Aortic Stenosis: Insights From the EVOLVED Randomized Clinical Trial.
articles.abstract: IMPORTANCE: Myocardial fibrosis burden has been associated with adverse clinical outcomes in symptomatic patients with aortic stenosis. OBJECTIVE: To determine whether midwall myocardial fibrosis burden is associated with adverse clinical outcomes in asymptomatic patients and whether those with more fibrosis derive greater benefit from early intervention. DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS: This post hoc analysis of a randomized clinical trial was conducted between August 2017 and October 2022. The trial took place at 24 cardiac centers across the United Kingdom and Australia. Participants included asymptomatic patients with severe aortic stenosis and midwall fibrosis on cardiac magnetic resonance. These data were analyzed from October 2024 through June 2025. INTERVENTION: Early intervention with transcatheter or surgical aortic valve replacement. MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES: Primary outcome was all-cause death or unplanned aortic stenosis-related hospitalization. Secondary outcomes included the individual components of the primary outcome. RESULTS: In 224 trial participants (mean [SD] age, 73 [9] years; 63 women and 161 men, and mean [SD] aortic valve peak velocity 4.3 [0.5] m per second) with a median follow-up of 42 months, fibrosis burden (per 1% increase) was associated with an increase in the primary end point (hazard ratio [HR], 1.23; 95% CI, 1.08-1.37) and its component of unplanned aortic stenosis-related hospitalizations (HR, 1.22; 95% CI, 1.03-1.40) but not all-cause death (HR, 1.17; 95% CI, 0.98-1.35). There were no interactions between randomization arm and the midwall fibrosis burden for the primary (P for interaction = .39) or secondary end points. In patients with high fibrosis burden above the median, the primary end point occurred in 12 of 59 (20%) of those randomized to early intervention and 17 of 53 (32%) of those randomized to guideline-directed conservative management (HR, 0.62; 95% CI, 0.29-1.28). For the individual components, all-cause death occurred in 9 (15%) and 10 (19%) patients, respectively (HR, 0.84; 95% CI, 0.33-2.07), and unplanned aortic stenosis-related hospitalization in 4 (7%) and 13 (25%) patients respectively (HR, 0.27; 95% CI, 0.08-0.77). In patients with low fibrosis burden below the median, there were no differences in the primary outcome (HR, 1.05; 95% CI, 0.39-2.86) or its components between intervention groups. CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: In this study, in asymptomatic patients with severe aortic stenosis, higher midwall fibrosis burden was associated with adverse outcomes. There was no demonstrable heterogeneity by the degree of midwall fibrosis for the treatment effects of early surgical or transcatheter aortic valve replacement compared to clinical surveillance. TRIAL REGISTRATION: ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT03094143.
articles.authors: Craig NJ, Loganath K, Everett RJ, Bing R, Ramtoola T et al.

articles.title: Early pannus after transcatheter heart valve implantation leading to delayed coronary obstruction: a case report.
articles.abstract: BACKGROUND: Coronary obstruction after transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI) is a rare complication typically occurring acutely. Delayed coronary obstruction (DCO) due to early pannus formation is exceptionally uncommon. CASE SUMMARY: A 74-year-old woman with no coronary artery disease underwent TAVI with a self-expandable 26-mm Evolut Pro + (Medtronic) transcatheter heart valve (THV) six months prior. She presented with recurrent non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarctions (NSTEMI), despite no angiographic evidence of acute coronary lesions. Multimodality imaging revealed early pannus overgrowth around the THV, causing mechanical obstruction of the left main ostium. Following Heart Team discussion, she underwent successful coronary artery bypass grafting without the need for surgical THV intervention. DISCUSSION: This case highlights a rare cause of DCO post-TAVI. Both orthotopic chimney stenting and THV explant with surgical valve replacement were deemed unsuitable in this context.
articles.authors: Perrard L, Lafont A, Akodad M, Nataf P, Gall E

articles.title: Evaluating the Efficacy and Outcomes of Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement Versus Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement in Low- to Intermediate-Risk Aortic Regurgitation Patients: A Comprehensive Clinical Comparison.
articles.abstract: BACKGROUND: Transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) has been established as an alternative to surgery for high-risk aortic regurgitation (AR) patients. However, its applicability to low- and intermediate-risk populations remains under investigation. This study evaluates the clinical outcomes, quality of care, and patient-centered implications of TAVR versus surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) in this population. METHODS: Between 2021 and 2024, clinical data were retrospectively analyzed from 70 AR patients at our center, including 37 who underwent TAVR and 33 who received SAVR with bioprosthetic valves. Baseline characteristics, perioperative metrics, and major clinical outcomes were assessed. International registry data (FRANCE-TAVI and ALIGN-AR) were utilized for external validity comparisons. RESULTS: Baseline characteristics were comparable between groups. TAVR was associated with shorter operation time (p = 0.001), reduced blood use (p < 0.001), and shorter hospital stays (p = 0.021). No 30-day mortality was observed in TAVR, whereas four deaths occurred in the SAVR group (p = 0.029). Conduction abnormalities differed, with complete left bundle branch block (CLBBB) more frequent in TAVR (p = 0.025) and complete right bundle branch block (CRBBB) in SAVR (p = 0.029). Despite its minimally invasive nature, ICU observation time remained similar (p = 0.339) due to perioperative complications. Economic analysis suggests potential cost savings with TAVR in specific scenarios. CONCLUSION: TAVR offers favorable short-term outcomes for low- to intermediate-risk AR patients, yet challenges in perioperative care require optimization. Long-term studies and multicenter trials are needed to refine patient selection and postprocedural management strategies.
articles.authors: Zhang X, Yan C, Li S, Cheng C

articles.title: Isolated Transcatheter and Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement in the Evolut Low-Risk Trial: 5-Year Comparative Outcomes
articles.abstract: We aim to compare the 5-year outcomes of patients who received isolated transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) versus those who underwent isolated surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) from the Evolut Low Risk Trial.
articles.authors: Basel Ramlawi, G. Michael Deeb, Steven J. Yakubov, Mubashir Mumtaz, Michael W.A. Chu, Pierre Berthoumieu, Shigeru Miyagawa, Yoojin Jung, John K. Forrest, Michael J. Reardon

surgical.commentary:
Today features several important comparisons. The under-65 TAVR utilization study (Glance et al., Annals of Thoracic Surgery) is the most concerning: a 2.7-fold variation in TAVR-vs-SAVR odds across hospitals suggests institutional culture — not patient anatomy or risk — is driving decisions in younger patients. When 46.5% of the lowest-risk patients under 65 receive TAVR despite both major guidelines recommending surgery, we are witnessing the kind of indication creep that Kaul, Bowdish, and others have warned about. The lifetime management implications are real: these patients will likely need reintervention, and the EXPLANTORREDO-TAVR data published today remind us that explant carries 12–16% mortality while redo-TAVR, though safer, creates progressive patient-prosthesis mismatch concerns.

The Evolut Low Risk 5-year isolated-procedure data (Ramlawi et al.) will need careful scrutiny for the specific outcomes in patients under 65 and under 70 — the populations where the SAVR-vs-TAVR debate is most consequential. The ESC 2025 shifted the TAVI-preferred threshold to ≥70 partly based on earlier Evolut data, but whether 5-year follow-up is sufficient to inform lifetime decisions remains the central unresolved question.

The single-center TAVR-vs-SAVR comparison for aortic regurgitation (Zhang et al.) shows favorable short-term TAVR metrics but is far too small and methodologically limited to shift practice. AR remains surgical territory, with TAVR only IIb for inoperable patients (ESC 2025). The ARTIST trial (JenaValve) is the study to watch here.

financial.label: Financial Analysis
financial.commentary:
The structural heart sector enters a consequential week with Abbott reporting earnings tomorrow (April 16), Boston Scientific on April 22, and Edwards Lifesciences on April 23. The timing concentrates investor attention on the three companies that collectively dominate the transcatheter valve landscape: Edwards (SAPIEN, PASCAL, Evoque), Abbott (MitraClip, TriClip, Navitor), and Boston Scientific (ACURATE, PASCAL competitor development).

The broader market context remains challenging. Tariff uncertainty and macroeconomic headwinds have weighed heavily on medtech — Boston Scientific's 34% six-month decline is the most dramatic, but Abbott is down ~20% from October peaks and Medtronic has shed ~7%. Edwards has been the relative outperformer (+7.6% over 6 months), likely reflecting its perceived structural heart purity and the regulatory tailwind from expanding TAVR and TTVR indications.

Two institutional holders (Diversify Advisory Services and Robeco Institutional Asset Management) reduced Edwards positions — routine portfolio rebalancing, but the direction is notable ahead of earnings. Edwards' forward P/E of 23.6x remains a premium to Medtronic (14.4x) and Abbott (16.6x), reflecting the market's willingness to pay for structural heart exposure — but that premium only holds if TAVR growth sustains and newer platforms (PASCAL, Evoque) deliver revenue.

  • FDA approval of Trilogy TAVR System for severe aortic regurgitation

Executive Summary

Today's most impactful finding comes from JAMA Cardiology: a post hoc analysis of the EVoLVeD trial shows that myocardial fibrosis burden predicts adverse outcomes in asymptomatic severe aortic stenosis patients, but — critically — the benefit of early intervention did not significantly differ based on fibrosis severity, complicating the narrative that fibrosis-guided referral can precisely identify who benefits most from early TAVR. Meanwhile, a major JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions cluster features the GASS-TAVR geriatric assessment score for predicting futility after TAVR, a NOTION-3 substudy showing PCI harms frail TAVR patients without benefit, and a landmark TVT Registry analysis of 5,401 mitral valve-in-valve cases revealing that low post-procedural gradients paradoxically predict worse survival. The Evolut Low Risk 5-year isolated-procedure data are published in Annals of Thoracic Surgery, and a troubling Annals study documents that nearly half of the lowest-surgical-risk patients under 65 are receiving TAVR — directly counter to both ACC/AHA and ESC guidelines.

For clinicians parsing today's data: the EVoLVeD fibrosis analysis reinforces the trend toward early intervention in asymptomatic AS (now ESC Class IIa) but reminds us that no single biomarker cleanly stratifies treatment benefit. The NOTION-3 frailty substudy adds nuance to the concomitant PCI-plus-TAVR strategy — a win in non-frail patients, a liability in frail ones. The mitral valve-in-valve gradient data upend conventional wisdom and demand a rethinking of post-MVIV echocardiographic benchmarks. And the under-65 TAVR utilization data should concern every Heart Team: practice is outpacing evidence in the very population where long-term durability matters most.

Today's Key Findings

[NOTABLE] EVoLVeD Fibrosis Analysis (JAMA Cardiology): In 224 asymptomatic severe AS patients with midwall fibrosis on CMR (median follow-up 42 months), higher fibrosis burden was associated with increased composite events (HR 1.23 per 1% increase, 95% CI 1.08–1.37), driven by unplanned AS-related hospitalizations. However, no significant interaction existed between fibrosis burden and randomization arm (P for interaction = 0.39). In the high-fibrosis subgroup, early intervention numerically reduced the primary endpoint (20% vs 32%) and significantly reduced hospitalizations (7% vs 25%, HR 0.27). In the low-fibrosis subgroup, no difference was seen. Editorial note: This is a post hoc subgroup analysis of a modest-sized trial — hypothesis-generating, not definitive. The absence of a statistically significant interaction means we cannot conclude that fibrosis selectively identifies responders to early intervention, despite the tantalizing point estimates. The ESC 2025 guidelines already reference EVoLVeD among the trials supporting early intervention (IIa) in asymptomatic severe AS, but fibrosis-guided patient selection remains unvalidated prospectively.

[NOTABLE] Hospital-Level Variation in TAVR vs SAVR in Patients Under 65 (Annals of Thoracic Surgery): Among 13,907 aortic valve replacements in adults under 65 (Vizient database, 2018–2023), a striking 55.8% received TAVR. Even among the lowest-risk patients (predicted mortality <0.5%), 46.5% underwent TAVR. Hospital-level variation was enormous (median odds ratio 2.69), suggesting non-clinical factors — institutional culture, referral patterns, volume incentives — are driving procedure choice in the very population where both ACC/AHA (<65 = SAVR, Class I) and ESC (<70, low risk = SAVR, Class I) recommend surgery. This mirrors concerns raised by Bowdish, Badhwar, and others about indication creep outpacing evidence, particularly given limited TAVR durability data beyond 10 years and the exclusion of younger patients from major RCTs.

[NOTABLE] Transmitral Gradients After Valve-in-Valve (JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions): In 5,401 patients from the STS/ACC TVT Registry undergoing transcatheter MVIV (2015–2024), low discharge transmitral gradient (<4 mmHg) was paradoxically associated with higher 3-year mortality compared to intermediate (4–7 mmHg; adjusted HR 1.52, P = 0.0002) and high gradients (>7 mmHg; adjusted HR 1.35, P = 0.01). Low-gradient patients had lower cardiac output and cardiac index, suggesting the gradient reflects hemodynamic status rather than valve function. The authors caution against using discharge gradient alone to judge procedural success — a direct challenge to current clinical reflexes.

[NOTABLE] NOTION-3 Substudy: PCI in Frail TAVR Patients (JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions): Among 407 NOTION-3 patients with available frailty data (32% frail), PCI reduced MACE, all-cause mortality, MI, and urgent revascularization in non-frail patients (HR 0.42, P < 0.001). In frail patients, PCI provided no benefit and increased bleeding (HR 2.51, P = 0.011). Caveat: Frailty was assessed post hoc using a calculated score, not a pre-specified validated instrument. The subgroup sizes are small (130 frail patients). These findings require prospective confirmation but suggest a "less is more" approach to revascularization in the frailest TAVR candidates.

Aortic Valve (TAVR/TAVI)

Evolut Low Risk 5-Year Isolated Procedure Outcomes

Ramlawi et al., Annals of Thoracic Surgery report 5-year outcomes comparing isolated TAVR versus isolated SAVR from the Evolut Low Risk trial. The abstract provided is limited, but this analysis is important because the original trial included patients with concomitant procedures — isolating TAVR-vs-SAVR removes confounders introduced by combined PCI or other interventions. These data join PARTNER 3 and DEDICATE in building the 5-year evidence base for low-risk TAVR, though the ESC 2025 guidelines already shifted the TAVI-preferred threshold to age ≥70 based in part on earlier Evolut follow-up. Notably, SAVR remains preferred below age 70 (ESC) or 65 (ACC/AHA) given unresolved durability questions beyond a decade.

GASS-TAVR: A Geriatric Assessment Score for Futility

Fumagalli et al. (JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions) developed and validated a geriatric assessment-based score predicting 1-year mortality or functional decline in 562 consecutive TAVR candidates ≥75 years across three Italian centers. The composite endpoint occurred in 13.9% of patients. Predictors included nutritional status (Mini Nutritional Assessment), baseline functional dependence (BADL), renal function, and pulmonary artery pressure. The score achieved an AUC of 0.92 in derivation and 0.87 in validation — substantially outperforming STS-PROM. This is a welcome addition to the futility prediction literature, though it requires external validation in non-Italian cohorts and prospective testing before clinical adoption. The accompanying editorial (Mattace-Raso) contextualizes the growing recognition that procedural success is necessary but not sufficient — functional benefit must be the benchmark.

Cerebral Embolic Protection in High-Calcium TAVR

Pawar et al. (JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions) examine the effectiveness of cerebral embolic protection devices during TAVR in patients with high aortic valve calcium burden. Full data are not available in the abstract, but this addresses an important question: whether CEP devices provide differential benefit in the highest-risk anatomy for embolization. Current data on CEP remain mixed (the PROTECTED-TAVR trial showed a signal for clinical stroke reduction but did not reach significance for the primary endpoint), and risk-stratified analyses like this may help identify patients who derive the most benefit.

Conduction Disturbances: Persistent Is What Matters

Okita et al. (International Journal of Cardiology) analyzed 1,007 TAVI patients across two centers and found that persistent conduction disturbances (present at discharge or beyond 7 days) — but not transient or pre-existing ones — independently predicted the composite of cardiovascular death or heart failure hospitalization at 2 years (log-rank P = 0.023). Persistent CD occurred in 11.1% of patients. This has practical implications for post-TAVI monitoring: transient conduction abnormalities that resolve before discharge may not warrant the same level of concern or pacemaker implantation, though prospective validation is needed before changing threshold-to-implant protocols.

Cardiac Tamponade After TAVI: A 12-Year Experience

Hussein et al. (International Journal of Cardiology) report on 29 tamponade events among 2,354 TAVI procedures at a single UK center (incidence 1.2%), with 51.7% mortality at 90 days. The incidence declined from 2.3% (2013–2018) to 0.9% (2019–2025, P = 0.004), driven mainly by elimination of RV perforation through LV wire pacing adoption. Annular rupture remained the dominant mechanism (51.7%) and trended toward highest mortality (66.7% at 90 days). All but one case involved balloon-expandable valves. These data underscore that while tamponade is rare and declining, annular rupture remains a devastating event — reinforcing the need for meticulous CT-based annular sizing, particularly as TAVR moves into lower-risk populations where complication tolerance is lower.

TAVR Reintervention: Failure Mechanism Does Not Predict Outcomes

Penta et al. (Circulation: Cardiovascular Interventions) report from the EXPLANTORREDO-TAVR registry (553 patients, 29 centers) that structural valve deterioration (SVD, 65%) and non-structural valve dysfunction (NSVD, 35%) had distinct profiles — SVD occurred later (50.7 vs 5.5 months), was more common in balloon-expandable valves, and favored non-BEV at redo-TAVR — but neither failure mechanism predicted differential mortality after redo-TAVR or TAVR-explant. Importantly, 30-day mortality for TAVR-explant remained high at 12–16% regardless of mechanism, consistent with prior data establishing explant as a high-risk procedure. Redo-TAVR mortality was substantially lower (1.7–3.2% at 30 days). These data inform lifetime management planning — a central concern of the ESC 2025 guidelines — and support the emerging preference for redo-TAVR when anatomically feasible.

Delayed Coronary Obstruction From Pannus Formation

Perrard et al. (European Heart Journal: Case Reports) describe a rare case of delayed coronary obstruction caused by early pannus overgrowth around a self-expandable THV (Evolut Pro+) just 6 months post-TAVI, causing recurrent NSTEMI. Neither chimney stenting nor explant was deemed feasible; the patient underwent CABG. This case highlights an underappreciated failure mode that may become more relevant as cumulative TAVR implant numbers grow and patients survive longer with their prostheses.

TAVR vs SAVR for Aortic Regurgitation: Early Single-Center Data

Zhang et al. (Cardiology Research and Practice) retrospectively compared 37 TAVR and 33 SAVR patients with aortic regurgitation at low-to-intermediate risk (single Chinese center, 2021–2024). TAVR showed shorter procedure times, less blood use, shorter stays, and zero 30-day mortality versus four SAVR deaths (P = 0.029). Major caveats: This is a tiny, non-randomized, single-center study with no mention of propensity matching or adjustment for selection bias. AR remains "Class IIb for inoperable patients" in ESC 2025, and the JenaValve ARTIST trial (NCT06608823) is the first RCT designed to compare TAVR versus SAVR for AR. Until ARTIST data are available, these results should be viewed as hypothesis-generating at best.

TAVI Expansion in India

ET HealthWorld reports on the expanding role of TAVI in India, framing it as a minimally invasive innovation redefining heart valve care. While TAVI adoption in emerging markets is an important access story, the same guideline principles apply: age-appropriate patient selection, Heart Team decision-making, and realistic expectations about durability in younger populations are essential regardless of geography.

Mitral Valve (MitraClip, PASCAL, TMVR)

The Gradient Paradox After Mitral Valve-in-Valve

The Abbas et al. TVT Registry analysis (detailed above) is the day's most clinically disruptive mitral finding. A low transmitral gradient (<4 mmHg) after MVIV reflects low cardiac output — not excellent valve function. The accompanying editorial by Pilgrim and Tomii contextualizes this "gradient paradox," arguing that cardiac output must be integrated into post-MVIV assessment. This parallels the well-known low-flow, low-gradient paradox in aortic stenosis and should change how echocardiographers report post-MVIV hemodynamics. The recent Heart Valve Collaboratory framework on imaging transcatheter valve failure is directly relevant here.

5-in-1 Transcatheter Mitral Valve Replacement

Camaj et al. (JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions) describe a combined transcatheter procedure involving mitral valve replacement with ventricular and atrial septal closure using electrosurgery. Abstract details are limited, but this reflects the growing complexity of catheter-based structural interventions and the trend toward addressing multiple pathologies in a single session — an approach that trades procedural efficiency against cumulative risk in what are typically high-risk patients.

APSC Consensus on TEER for Mitral and Tricuspid Regurgitation

Yeo et al. (JACC: Asia) present updated Asian Pacific Society of Cardiology consensus statements on transcatheter edge-to-edge repair for both MR and TR. Sixteen consensus statements were developed using GRADE methodology. This is notable as a regional adaptation effort — the Asia-Pacific region has distinct epidemiology (more rheumatic disease, younger patients, variable access to surgery) that may appropriately shift the risk-benefit calculus for TEER, though the same evidence limitations apply.

Tricuspid Valve (TriClip, TTVR)

Real-World Evoque TTVR Data: Reassuring but Early

TCTMD reports that real-world Evoque transcatheter tricuspid valve replacement data are "reassuring" — consistent with the recently published STS/ACC TVT Registry analysis showing 98.4% implantation success, 3.1% 30-day mortality, and significant functional improvement. The headline captures the right nuance: it's still early days. The ESC 2025 guidelines gave transcatheter TR treatment a Class IIa recommendation based on TRILUMINATE, Tri.Fr, and TRISCEND II, but long-term durability and the optimal threshold for intervening in TR (repair vs replacement) remain unresolved. New CIED implantation (15.9%) and bleeding (7.9%) rates in the TVT Registry were actually lower than the TRISCEND II pivotal trial, which is encouraging but may reflect real-world patient selection differences.

Acute Hemodynamic Instability After TTVR

JACC: Cardiovascular Interventions publishes on the frequency and outcomes of acute hemodynamic instability following TTVR — a complication unique to transcatheter tricuspid replacement that has garnered attention as volumes increase. The right ventricle is preload-sensitive, and abruptly eliminating severe TR can unmask RV dysfunction. This remains an active area of investigation and underscores the importance of pre-procedural RV assessment — the ESC 2025 guidelines explicitly exclude patients with severe RV dysfunction from transcatheter TR treatment recommendations.

Surgical vs. Transcatheter Comparisons

Today features several important comparisons. The under-65 TAVR utilization study (Glance et al., Annals of Thoracic Surgery) is the most concerning: a 2.7-fold variation in TAVR-vs-SAVR odds across hospitals suggests institutional culture — not patient anatomy or risk — is driving decisions in younger patients. When 46.5% of the lowest-risk patients under 65 receive TAVR despite both major guidelines recommending surgery, we are witnessing the kind of indication creep that Kaul, Bowdish, and others have warned about. The lifetime management implications are real: these patients will likely need reintervention, and the EXPLANTORREDO-TAVR data published today remind us that explant carries 12–16% mortality while redo-TAVR, though safer, creates progressive patient-prosthesis mismatch concerns.

The Evolut Low Risk 5-year isolated-procedure data (Ramlawi et al.) will need careful scrutiny for the specific outcomes in patients under 65 and under 70 — the populations where the SAVR-vs-TAVR debate is most consequential. The ESC 2025 shifted the TAVI-preferred threshold to ≥70 partly based on earlier Evolut data, but whether 5-year follow-up is sufficient to inform lifetime decisions remains the central unresolved question.

The single-center TAVR-vs-SAVR comparison for aortic regurgitation (Zhang et al.) shows favorable short-term TAVR metrics but is far too small and methodologically limited to shift practice. AR remains surgical territory, with TAVR only IIb for inoperable patients (ESC 2025). The ARTIST trial (JenaValve) is the study to watch here.

Device & Technology

Vela Martín et al. (Journal of Invasive Cardiology) report a case of catastrophic right coronary artery dissection after surgical removal of coronary chimney stents following valve-in-valve TAVI. While a single case report, it illustrates the cascading complexity of managing failed transcatheter valves — each reintervention layer adds risk. The ESC 2025 guidelines emphasize that lifetime management planning at the index procedure is paramount, including assessment of future coronary access. This case is a cautionary example of what happens when that planning falls short.

A new trial (NCT07524595) will compare the self-expanding Hydra THV series versus balloon-expandable devices (SAPIEN, MyVal) for valve-in-valve TAVI in failed surgical bioprostheses — addressing the growing question of optimal valve-in-valve strategy, including the supra-annular design advantages of self-expanding platforms for avoiding patient-prosthesis mismatch.

Industry & Market

Boston Scientific announced an $88.4M (€75M) investment to expand its R&D campus in Galway, Ireland — a strategic hub for the company's structural heart portfolio, including the ACURATE neo2 TAVR platform and PASCAL TEER system. The Galway Daily reported the investment will create new engineering positions focused on next-generation devices. This is consistent with BSX's aggressive structural heart expansion strategy, even as its stock faces significant headwinds (down ~34% over 6 months).

Financial Analysis

The structural heart sector enters a consequential week with Abbott reporting earnings tomorrow (April 16), Boston Scientific on April 22, and Edwards Lifesciences on April 23. The timing concentrates investor attention on the three companies that collectively dominate the transcatheter valve landscape: Edwards (SAPIEN, PASCAL, Evoque), Abbott (MitraClip, TriClip, Navitor), and Boston Scientific (ACURATE, PASCAL competitor development).

The broader market context remains challenging. Tariff uncertainty and macroeconomic headwinds have weighed heavily on medtech — Boston Scientific's 34% six-month decline is the most dramatic, but Abbott is down ~20% from October peaks and Medtronic has shed ~7%. Edwards has been the relative outperformer (+7.6% over 6 months), likely reflecting its perceived structural heart purity and the regulatory tailwind from expanding TAVR and TTVR indications.

Two institutional holders (Diversify Advisory Services and Robeco Institutional Asset Management) reduced Edwards positions — routine portfolio rebalancing, but the direction is notable ahead of earnings. Edwards' forward P/E of 23.6x remains a premium to Medtronic (14.4x) and Abbott (16.6x), reflecting the market's willingness to pay for structural heart exposure — but that premium only holds if TAVR growth sustains and newer platforms (PASCAL, Evoque) deliver revenue.
